name: Hebrew
notes: Modern Israeli Hebrew (Non-Oriental pronunciation, Laufer 1999). Consonantal abjad — vowel diacritics (niqqud) are normally omitted in adult text, so all niqqud combining marks are declared optional_marks: an unpointed skeleton is UNDERSPECIFIED input (exactly like undiacritized Arabic in ar.json), while pointed text is transcribed fully. NIQQUD (Modern reading values, 5-vowel system /a e i o u/ with no phonemic length — Laufer 1999 p.98 vowel chart; Bolozky 1997): patach ַ and qamats (gadol) ָ = /a/; tsere ֵ and segol ֶ = /e/ (the Tiberian qamats/patach and tsere/segol quality distinctions are merged in Modern Hebrew); hiriq ִ = /i/; holam ֹ = /o/; qubuts ֻ = /u/; qamats qatan ׇ (U+05C7) = /o/ and plain qamats carries /o/ as a second-ranked candidate for the qamats-qatan syllables ordinary type does not distinguish (e.g. כָּל /kol/); hataf-segol ֱ /e/, hataf-patach ֲ /a/, hataf-qamats ֳ /o/. SHVA ְ: Modern Hebrew realises shva as either zero (shva nach and most historical shva-na positions: זְמַן /zman/) or /e/ (clusters of rising sonority difficulty and the prefixes בְּ/וְ/לְ: /be/, /ve/, /le/); the choice is morphology- and sonority-conditioned (Bolozky 1997) and not recoverable from the mark alone, so both candidates are listed with zero ranked first. MATRES LECTIONIS: holam male וֹ = /o/, shuruq וּ = /u/, hiriq male ִי = /i/, tsere male ֵי = /e/ (Modern Hebrew reads ֵי as plain /e/; Laufer 1999 p.98 treats the Non-Oriental [ei̯] variant as a vowel sequence). In unpointed ktiv male spelling a medial ו stands for /o/ or /u/ (consonantal /v/ is conventionally doubled וו) and a medial י for /i/ or /j/, so the positional tables rank the vowel readings first medially/finally but keep /v/, /j/ word-initially (the conjunction ו־ /ve/, prefix י־). DAGESH ּ: Modern Hebrew has NO gemination (dagesh forte is mute — Bolozky 1997; Laufer 1999 notes gemination survives only for some Oriental speakers), and of the historical begadkefat set only ב~v, כ~χ, פ~f still alternate with their stop values בּ /b/, כּ /k/, פּ /p/; dagesh in ג/ד/ת changes nothing (the historical fricatives [ɣ ð θ] merged into [ɡ d t]), so the bare mark maps to zero and only the letter+dagesh digraphs that matter are declared (בּ כּ פּ ךּ, plus תּ/יּ/הּ as explicit no-ops for the commonest carriers). SHIN/SIN DOTS: שׁ /ʃ/, שׂ /s/; undotted ש keeps both candidates, /ʃ/ first (more frequent). GUTTURALS: א and ע are /ʔ/ or zero for Non-Oriental speakers (Laufer 1999: in the Non-Oriental pronunciation /ʕ/ merges with /ʔ/ and /ħ/ with /χ/; the Oriental /ʕ ħ/ are not modeled in this leaf); ח = /χ/; ה = /h/ (elidable; silent word-finally as a mater for /a/ or /e/), הּ with mapiq is a real /h/. FURTIVE PATACH (patach gnuva): a word-final ח or ע bearing patach is read vowel-BEFORE-consonant: רוּחַ /ʁuaχ/, שׁוֹמֵעַ /ʃomea/ — modeled with the positional digraphs חַ/עַ word_final. RESH = /ʁ/ (uvular; Laufer 1999: usually a uvular approximant for Non-Orientals), alveolar [r] kept as a second candidate/allophone for Oriental speech. Maqaf ־, meteg ֽ and rafe ֿ are phonically null. KNOWN LIMITATION: unpointed text (the norm) omits most vowels; like ar.json this spec does no morphological vowel restoration, so unpointed transcriptions are consonant skeletons plus matres lectionis. The registered WikiPron gold heb_hebr_broad.tsv is unpointed and additionally mixes transcription traditions (a minority of rows are Tiberian-style: ɔː, ə, ŋ), so a high PER floor is structural there, not a spec bug. UNICODE MARK ORDER: real-world pointed Hebrew is inconsistently normalized — typed convention puts dagesh/shin-dot before the vowel point, but Unicode canonical ordering (NFC/NFD) sorts the vowel (ccc 10-20) before dagesh (ccc 21) and the shin/sin dots (ccc 24/25). The spec therefore declares BOTH orders: letter+dagesh and letter+vowel+dagesh digraphs for בּ/כּ/פּ, and letter+dot plus letter+vowel+dot digraphs for שׁ/שׂ, with the bare dots mapping to zero as a residual fallback. These are written-cluster contexts, not products: each names one letter+pointing combination attested in normalized text.

sources.author: Laufer, A.
sources.title: Hebrew (Illustrations of the IPA)
sources.publisher: Handbook of the International Phonetic Association, Cambridge University Press, pp. 96-99; earlier version in Journal of the International Phonetic Association 20(2), 1990, 40-43
sources.notes: Primary IPA illustration of Modern Israeli Hebrew, Oriental and Non-Oriental. Consulted for: the consonant chart (p.96: p b t d k ɡ ʔ, m n, r, f v s z ʃ ʒ χ h, plus ħ ʕ in Oriental speech only) and its minimal-pair word list (p.97); the 5-vowel system i e a o u (p.98 חִיל/חֵיל/חַל/חוֹל/חוּל series); distinctive stress (ˈbereχ 'knee' vs beˈreχ 'he blessed'); the Non-Oriental mergers ʕ→ʔ and ħ→χ; resh as a uvular approximant [ʁ] for Non-Orientals vs alveolar trill [r] for Orientals; gemination surviving only in (some) Oriental speech; the affricate /ts/ צ; ʔ/h elidable especially in unstressed syllables. Example transcriptions harvested into data/gold/primary_sources (rows laufer1999-*).

sources.author: Bolozky, S.
sources.title: Israeli Hebrew Phonology
sources.publisher: In A.S. Kaye (ed.), Phonologies of Asia and Africa, Vol. 1, pp. 287-311. Winona Lake: Eisenbrauns
sources.notes: Consulted for: the Modern (Israeli) Hebrew 5-vowel phoneme system with no length or gemination contrast; the reduction of the begadkefat alternation to the three pairs b~v, k~χ, p~f (t/d/ɡ no longer alternate); shva realisation as zero or [e] conditioned by morphology and cluster sonority; milra (final) default stress with lexical milel exceptions (segolates).

sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.title: Modern Hebrew phonology
sources.notes: Secondary source kept alongside the primaries: confirms the 5-vowel system, the phonemicised begadkefat pairs, resh as uvular [ʁ̞], ayin absorbed by /ʔ/ in non-Oriental speech, the ħ→χ merger, orthographic shva pronounced /e/ in the prefixes ve-/be- and silent in e.g. זְמַן, and the milra/milel stress patterns.

stress.notes: Modern Israeli Hebrew default is milra (final stress, -1); stress is distinctive (Laufer 1999 p.98: ˈbereχ 'knee' vs beˈreχ 'he blessed'). Milel (penultimate) is common in a substantial minority of words (especially segolate nouns: מֶלֶךְ, שֶׁמֶשׁ) and cannot be predicted from unvocalized orthography. With niqqud the stressed syllable is identifiable; without niqqud (the norm in adult text) stress placement is not reliably computable from orthography alone. This block models the milra default only. Sources: Laufer (1999), Bolozky (1997).

ancestors.notes: Canaanite (Northwest Semitic), a branch of Central Semitic.
Aramaic adstrate: dominant vernacular of Second-Temple Judea; heavy lexical/phonological layer.
Yiddish adstrate on Revived (Modern) Hebrew: European-substrate phonology of the first speakers.
Arabic adstrate: shared Semitic areal features and modern loanwords.

location.notes: Glottolog's representative point for Modern Hebrew.
